risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Subjects were then randomised 1:1 using a computer-generated randomisation list using randomisation blocks of four." Comment: The study explicitly describes a proper random sequence generation method (computer-generated randomisation list with block randomisation), which supports truly random allocation.

Quote: "At screening, eligible subjects were sequentially assigned a unique three-digit patient identification number. Subjects were then randomised 1:1 using a computer-generated randomisation list using randomisation blocks of four. These blocks were assigned to study centres. Within each centre the allocation of a randomisation number was linked in a chronologically ascending manner to the sequence of arrival of the patients at the study centre." Comment: This describes sequence generation and assignment order, but does not explicitly describe any concealment mechanism (e.g. central randomisation, pharmacy control, or sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes) that would prevent the person enrolling participants from foreseeing the next assignment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "This was a Phase III, multicentre, open-label, randomised, active-controlled, parallel-group study..." and "Pain severity was self-assessed on a daily basis using a verbal rating scale..." Comment: The study was explicitly open-label, so participants and personnel were not blinded. The main outcomes (days with dysmenorrhoeic pain, pain severity, interference with daily activities, user satisfaction) are subjective and plausibly influenced by knowledge of the assigned regimen. Therefore performance bias is at high risk.

Quote: "This was a Phase III, multicentre, open-label, randomised, active-controlled, parallel-group study..." and "The primary efficacy variable was the number of days with dysmenorrhoeic pain over 140 days... recorded in a daily diary... Pain severity was self-assessed on a daily basis using a verbal rating scale..." and "Secondary efficacy variables included... investigator-assessed global improvement in dysmenorrhoea... and user satisfaction..." Comment: Outcome assessors included the patient (self-reported pain diary, satisfaction) and the investigator (global improvement). Because the trial was explicitly open-label, these assessors were not blinded to allocation. The study includes subjective, judgement-dependent outcomes, especially self-reported pain, so knowledge of allocation could readily influence outcome measurement. By the worst-outcome rule, the presence of unblinded subjective outcomes makes this domain High risk overall.

Quote: "A total of 223 patients who received study medication were included in the FAS (flexibleMIB regimen, n=115; conventional regimen, n=108) and 210 patients (110 and 100, respectively) completed the study as planned (Figure 1)." Quote: "All women who took at least one unit of study medication and for whom at least one observation after dosing was available were included in the full analysis set (FAS)." Comment: Post-randomization loss by the end of the 140-day study was low and fairly balanced between groups: 5/115 (4.3%) in the flexibleMIB arm and 8/108 (7.4%) in the conventional arm, an absolute difference of about 3 percentage points. This is well below thresholds that would usually suggest important attrition bias, and the primary results are reported over the 140-day treatment period in the FAS.
